title: Choosing a Client Access Type
description:
## Configuring a Client for a Single-Page App

You are registering a **browser-based single-page application (SPA)** — JavaScript running in the user's browser — as a client in Keycloak. The code is fully downloaded to the browser, so it **cannot keep any secret confidential**.

Which client configuration is correct?

options.text: **Confidential** client with a client secret embedded in the JavaScript bundle.
**Public** client using the Authorization Code flow with **PKCE**.
**Bearer-only** client, so it can initiate the login redirect.
**Confidential** client using the Client Credentials grant for each user.

explanation: A SPA's source ships to the browser, so it **cannot hold a client secret** — that rules out a confidential configuration. The correct choice is a **public** client (no secret) using the **Authorization Code flow with PKCE**, which protects the flow against authorization-code interception without needing a secret. Option **a** is insecure: a secret baked into client-side JavaScript is trivially extractable, so it provides no protection. Option **c** is wrong because a **bearer-only** client *only validates tokens* (typical for an API) and can never initiate a login. Option **d** misuses the Client Credentials grant, which is for machine-to-machine access with **no user** — it can't authenticate the human using the SPA.

hints.title: Can it keep a secret?
hints.body: Confidential = server-side app that can store a secret. Public = browser/mobile app that can't. Bearer-only = an API that just checks tokens.
